questions.text:
Consider the Red-Black tree depicted below. If we insert node 58, which sequence of operations is required to maintain the Red-Black properties?

Note: a node in **bold** is black and an underlined node is red.

```mermaid
graph TD
    30((30)):::black --- 15((15)):::black
    30 --- 45((45)):::red
    45 --- 35((35)):::black
    45 --- 60((60)):::black
    60 --- 55((55)):::red
    
    classDef black fill:#333,stroke:#000,color:#fff,font-weight:bold;
    classDef red fill:#f96,stroke:#f00,color:#000,text-decoration:underline;
```

choices.label: A
choices.text: Recolor parent and uncle, then recolor grandparent.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Perform a left rotation on node 55, then a right rotation on node 60.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Perform a right rotation on node 60, then recolor.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No operations required; the tree remains valid.

questions.explanation: Node 58 is inserted as the right child of 55. Node 55 is red, and its uncle (right child of 60) is NIL (black). This forms a 'triangle' configuration (60 \(\to\) 55-left \(\to\) 58-right). To fix this, Case 2 is invoked: a left rotation on the parent (55) to form a 'line', followed by Case 3: a right rotation on the grandparent (60) and recoloring.

questions.text: Given the array representation of a tree \( A = \{50, 30, 40, 20, 10, 25, 35\} \), which index contains the first element that violates the **Max-Heap** property?

choices.label: D
choices.text: None, it is already a max-heap

questions.explanation:
Checking parents (1-based): 
- Root (50) > children (30, 40). 
- Node 30 > children (20, 10). 
- Node 40 > children (25, 35). 
All parent nodes are greater than or equal to their children. Thus, no violations occur.

questions.text: For the recurrence \( T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^2 \log n \), determine the asymptotic bound using the Master Theorem.
questions.explanation: Here \( a=4, b=2 \). \( n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 4} = n^2 \). Since \( f(n) = n^2 \log n \), we compare it to \( n^{\log_b a} \). This is Case 2 of the Master Theorem (extended), where \( f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^k n) \) with \( k=1 \). The solution is \( T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k+1} n) = \Theta(n^2 \log^2 n) \).

questions.text: In a Red-Black tree with \( n \) internal nodes, what is the maximum possible height \( h \) in terms of the number of nodes?
questions.explanation: A fundamental property of Red-Black trees is that a tree with \( n \) internal nodes has height \( h \leq 2 \log_2(n+1) \), ensuring logarithmic time complexity for operations.

The outer loop runs for \( i = 1, 2, 4, ..., 2^{\lfloor \log n \rfloor} \). The inner loop runs \( i \) times. The total work is the sum of a geometric series: \( 1 + 2 + 4 + ... + n \approx 2n \). Thus, the complexity is \( O(n) \).

questions.text: If you perform a `MAX-HEAPIFY` on index 1 of the array \( A = \{15, 60, 45, 50, 30, 70, 80\} \), which value will end up at index 6?
questions.explanation:
1. Root is 15. Children are 60 and 45. Largest is 60 (idx 2). Swap 15 and 60. 
2. New root idx 2 (value 15). Children are 50 and 30. Largest is 50 (idx 4). Swap 15 and 50. 
3. No further swaps for 15. 
Note: Index 6 (value 70) was never touched because the heapify path followed the left branch (idx 2 then idx 4). Thus, the value at index 6 remains 70. Wait, 45 is at index 3. Let's re-verify: Index 6 is 70. Choice B is correct.

questions.text: What is the result of the `PARTITION` procedure on \( A = \langle 5, 8, 1, 3, 7, 9, 2 \rangle \) using the last element as the pivot (Lomuto scheme)?
Consider a Binary Search Tree where the root is 50. After inserting 30, 70, 20, 40, 60, and 80, what is the Pre-order traversal of the tree?
questions.explanation: BST structure: Root 50. Left: 30 (L: 20, R: 40). Right: 70 (L: 60, R: 80). Pre-order: Root-Left-Right: 50, 30, 20, 40, 70, 60, 80.

questions.text: For the recurrence \( T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + cn \), what is the total work done at the second level (level 1) of the recursion tree?
questions.explanation:
Level 0: \( cn \). 
Level 1: The nodes are \( T(n/3) \) and \( T(2n/3) \). The work is \( c(n/3) + c(2n/3) = cn \). Every level of this recursion tree sums to \( cn \) until the branches start hitting base cases.

questions.text: A Red-Black tree has a black-height of 3. What is the maximum possible number of internal nodes in this tree?
questions.explanation: A node with black-height \( bh \) has at least \( 2^{bh}-1 \) internal nodes. To maximize nodes, we alternate red and black nodes. Maximum height \( h = 2bh = 6 \). Max nodes \( n = 2^{h+1}-1 = 2^7-1 = 127 \)? No, black height property says \( n \geq 2^{bh}-1 \). Maximum nodes for black-height \( bh \) occurs when we maximize red nodes: \( 2^{2bh}-1 \). For \( bh=3 \), max nodes = \( 2^{2(3)}-1 = 2^6-1 = 63 \).

questions.text:
What is the predecessor of node 35 in the Red-Black tree shown below?

```mermaid
graph TD
    30((30)):::black --- 15((15)):::black
    30 --- 45((45)):::red
    45 --- 35((35)):::black
    45 --- 60((60)):::black
    60 --- 55((55)):::red
    
    classDef black fill:#333,stroke:#000,color:#fff,font-weight:bold;
    classDef red fill:#f96,stroke:#f00,color:#000,text-decoration:underline;
```
questions.explanation: In a BST, the predecessor is the largest value in the left subtree. Node 35 has no left subtree. Therefore, the predecessor is the first ancestor that contains 35 in its right subtree. Tracking up from 35: 45 (35 is in left), 30 (35 is in right). Thus, 30 is the predecessor.

questions.text: Which of the following is true regarding the asymptotic relationship between \( f(n) = n^2 \) and \( g(n) = n^{2.1} \)?
questions.explanation: \( \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n^2}{n^{2.1}} = \lim_{n \to \infty} n^{-0.1} = 0 \). This implies \( f(n) = o(g(n)) \), meaning \( f(n) = O(g(n)) \) but not \( \Theta(g(n)) \).

questions.text: When building a max-heap from the array \( \langle 4, 1, 3, 2, 16, 9, 10, 14, 8, 7 \rangle \), what is the state of the array after the first call to `MAX-HEAPIFY` (at the first non-leaf node)?
questions.explanation:
Non-leaf nodes start from index \( \lfloor n/2 \rfloor = 5 \). 
1. Index 5 (16): Children at 10 (7). 16 > 7, no swap. 
2. Index 4 (2): Children at 8 (14) and 9 (8). Largest is 14. Swap 2 and 14. 
Array becomes: [4, 1, 3, 14, 16, 9, 10, 2, 8, 7].

questions.text:
What is the black-height of the root (30) in the provided tree after inserting 50 and rebalancing?

```mermaid
graph TD
    30((30)):::black --- 15((15)):::black
    30 --- 45((45)):::red
    45 --- 35((35)):::black
    45 --- 60((60)):::black
    60 --- 55((55)):::red
    
    classDef black fill:#333,stroke:#000,color:#fff,font-weight:bold;
    classDef red fill:#f96,stroke:#f00,color:#000,text-decoration:underline;
```
questions.explanation: Inserting 50 into this tree triggers Case 2 (triangle) then Case 3 (line) at the node 60 level. This involves rotations and recoloring within the subtree rooted at 45. Rebalancing a Case 2/3 violation does not change the black-height of the root unless Case 1 propagates to the root and forces a root-recolor. Here, 45 stays red or becomes black, but the path count of black nodes remains 2.

questions.text: In Quick Sort, if the partition always splits the array in a ratio of 9:1, what is the resulting time complexity?
questions.explanation: Any constant ratio split (even 99:1) results in a recursion tree depth of \( \log_{10/9} n \), which is still \( O(\log n) \). Since each level takes \( O(n) \) work, the total complexity is \( O(n \log n) \).

questions.text: Given the Insertion Sort algorithm, which input array results in the maximum number of comparisons?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An array sorted in descending order.

choices.label: B
choices.text: An array sorted in ascending order.

choices.label: C
choices.text: An array with all identical elements.

choices.label: D
choices.text: An array where elements are randomly distributed.

questions.explanation: The worst case for Insertion Sort is an array sorted in reverse (descending) order. Each new element must be compared and shifted past all previously sorted elements, leading to \( O(n^2) \) comparisons.

questions.text: In a max-heap with \( n \) nodes, where is the second-largest element located?

choices.label: D
choices.text: The rightmost node of the last level

questions.explanation: In a max-heap, the largest element is at the root (index 1). The second-largest must be one of the root's children (index 2 or index 3).

questions.text: What is the black-height of a NIL node in a Red-Black tree?
questions.explanation: By definition, the black-height of a node is the number of black nodes on the path to a leaf, NOT including the node itself. Since a NIL node is a leaf and has no descendants, its black-height is 0.

questions.text: Consider the following recurrence: \( T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \). Using the recursion tree, what is the total work done at level \( k \)?
questions.explanation: At level \( k \), there are \( 2^k \) nodes, each processing a subproblem of size \( n/2^k \). The work per node is \( f(n/2^k) = n/2^k \). Total work = \( 2^k \cdot (n/2^k) = n \).

questions.text: Which of the following properties is NOT required for a valid Red-Black tree?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The root must be red if it has two black children.

choices.label: C
choices.text: If a node is red, both its children are black.

choices.label: D
choices.text: For each node, all paths to descendant leaves contain the same number of black nodes.

questions.explanation: The root of a Red-Black tree must always be black. Option A is factually incorrect and not a property.

questions.text: A max-heap contains the elements \( \langle 100, 19, 36, 17, 3, 25, 1, 2, 7 \rangle \). After deleting the root (100) and performing `MAX-HEAPIFY`, what is the new root?
questions.explanation:
1. Replace root with the last element (7). 
2. Root is 7, children are 19 and 36. 
3. 36 is larger, swap 7 and 36. New root is 36.

questions.text: What is the worst-case time complexity of searching for a value in a Red-Black tree with \( n \) nodes?
questions.explanation: Because a Red-Black tree is always balanced (height is logarithmic), searching for a value takes \( O(h) \), which is \( O(\log n) \).

questions.text: In the Quick Sort `PARTITION` algorithm, what happens if all elements in the array are identical?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The pivot splits the array into one element and \( n-1 \) elements (worst case).

choices.label: B
choices.text: The pivot splits the array exactly in half (best case).

choices.label: D
choices.text: The partition procedure takes \( O(n \log n) \) time.

questions.explanation: In the Lomuto partition scheme, if all elements are equal to the pivot, the loop condition \( A[j] \leq x \) is always true. The index \( i \) will increment in every iteration, and the pivot will ultimately be swapped with the last element, resulting in a split of \( n-1 \) and 0 elements.

questions.text: Compare \( f(n) = 2^n \) and \( g(n) = 3^n \). Which of the following is true?
questions.explanation: \( \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{2^n}{3^n} = \lim_{n \to \infty} (2/3)^n = 0 \). Thus \( f(n) = o(g(n)) \), which implies \( f(n) = O(g(n)) \).

questions.text: A heap with 511 nodes is perfectly full. What is the height of a node at level 7 (where the root is level 0)?
questions.explanation: A full tree with 511 nodes has \( 2^9 - 1 \) nodes, meaning its levels are 0 through 8. Total height \( H = 8 \). The height of a node at level \( d \) is \( H - d = 8 - 7 = 1 \).

questions.text:
What is the post-order traversal of the following tree?

```mermaid
graph TD
    30((30)):::black --- 15((15)):::black
    30 --- 45((45)):::red
    45 --- 35((35)):::black
    45 --- 60((60)):::black

    classDef black fill:#333,stroke:#000,color:#fff,font-weight:bold;
    classDef red fill:#f96,stroke:#f00,color:#000,text-decoration:underline;
```
questions.explanation:
Post-order: Left-Right-Root. 
1. Left subtree of 30: **15**. 
2. Right subtree of 30 (rooted at 45): 
   - Left of 45: **35**. 
   - Right of 45: **60**. 
   - Root of subtree: **45**. 
3. Root of tree: **30**. 
Sequence: 15, 35, 60, 45, 30.

questions.text: If \( T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \), how many nodes are at level \( i \) in the recursion tree?
questions.explanation: In the standard recursion tree for \( aT(n/b) + f(n) \), each level \( i \) contains \( a^i \) nodes. Here \( a=2 \), so there are \( 2^i \) nodes.

questions.text: Which sorting algorithm is stable and has a worst-case time complexity of \( O(n \log n) \)?
questions.explanation: Merge Sort is stable and guaranteed \( O(n \log n) \). Quick Sort is \( O(n^2) \) worst-case and unstable. Heap Sort is \( O(n \log n) \) but unstable.

questions.text: What is the maximum number of edges on a path from the root to a leaf in a Red-Black tree with black-height \( bh \)?
questions.explanation: Since red nodes cannot be adjacent, the longest possible path alternates between red and black nodes. For a path with \( bh \) black nodes, the maximum total number of nodes is \( 2bh \) (including red nodes).

questions.text: What is the value of \( T(n) \) for the recurrence \( T(n) = T(n-1) + 1 \)?
questions.explanation: This is a simple arithmetic progression. Each step reduces \( n \) by 1 and adds a constant. Summing 1, \( n \) times, gives \( T(n) = \Theta(n) \).
